fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap the dart_style formatter into a little adapter layer so other tools can drive it over stdin/stdout with JSON. Basically something reads a JSON blob in, calls the formatter, and spits out the result. The tricky part is we want this to feel clean — not just one giant script doing everything. Think about how we separated concerns in that old analysis pipeline we built, where the I/O layer never touched the business logic directly.

The formatter itself needs to handle whole files and also smaller snippets differently — files get a trailing newline, snippets don't. Width settings and the style version (old vs new dart style) should affect how arguments and collections get split across lines. Line endings should either follow what's already in the source or be overridable. There's also some trailing comma behavior that affects whether things stay on one line or split.

When things go wrong — bad syntax, unsafe output — we need a clean, consistent error format that doesn't leak any runtime internals like stack traces or exception class names. Also, editor-style selection range tracking needs to work, and there's a utility for splitting source into before/selected/after segments that needs proper validation.

We need a test runner script that can point at a folder of JSON case files and run all of them automatically. Make sure the whole thing is properly structured, not a god file.

One quick follow-up from the questions that came in: for success cases, `scope=file` needs to end with exactly one trailing `\n`, and `scope=statement` must not append any file-level trailing newline. Same idea as before, just calling out that this is strict. Also, if the source starts with a comment like `// @dart=X.Y`, that takes precedence over the `language_version` coming in through JSON, and both parsing behavior and formatting style should follow the version from that comment whenever it exists.

A couple other defaults to keep in mind: if `page_width` isn’t present in the input, we should use dart_style’s default of 80 for layout decisions. The optional `uri` can be passed through as the source file URI too. It doesn’t directly change the formatted output, but it can show up in diagnostics and gives the underlying formatter more context.

On style behavior, language version < 3.7 uses the old Dart style, which means 4-space argument indentation and no trailing comma on split argument lists. Language version >= 3.7 uses the new style, which means 2-space indentation and a trailing comma after the last argument when split. For line endings, if `line_ending` is explicitly set in the JSON input, every formatter-produced line break should use that exact string. If it’s omitted, detect the first line ending already present in the source and use that consistently. If there isn’t one in the source either, fall back to `\n`.

And just to underline the structure piece since that came up too: keep the JSON adapter on stdin/stdout physically separate from the core formatting domain. The adapter shouldn’t blur into the formatter logic, and the core piece should depend only on abstractions per DIP, same spirit as the separation we wanted from the start.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When `trailing_commas` is set to `preserve`, any construct in the source that already has a trailing comma must be forced to multiline layout and must retain that trailing comma. Constructs without a trailing comma are still subject to normal width-based splitting rules and may remain compact if they fit.

When `selection_start` and `selection_length` are provided, stdout must follow this exact structure: `text<<END
<formatted text>
END
selection_start=<n>
selection_length=<n>
`. Offsets are zero-based character positions in the formatted output adjusted around the original selection.

The `source_segments` operation always outputs three lines: `before=...`, `selected=...`, `after=...`. When no selection range is given, `before` contains the entire source and `selected` and `after` are empty strings. Invalid or out-of-bounds ranges produce `error=invalid_selection` with `field` and `reason` lines instead.

Parse errors must be reported to stdout as: `error=parse_error
count=<number>
lines=<comma-separated line numbers>
`. The output must never contain exception class names, stack traces, or runtime-specific message text. A formatter safety failure where non-whitespace content would change is reported as `error=non_whitespace_output_change
`.

The test harness entry point is `bash rcb_tests/test.sh`. It reads all `*.json` files from the default case directory `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/`. An optional `--cases-dir <subdir>` argument redirects it to a different subdirectory under `rcb_tests/`.

The JSON stdin/stdout adapter must be physically and logically separated from core formatting logic. The core domain must never import or depend on JSON parsing, stdin, or stdout. The adapter translates JSON fields into idiomatic method calls on the core API only.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: Language version threshold at 3.7: versions below 3.7 use 4-space indent with no trailing comma (old style); versions 3.7 and above use 2-space indent with trailing comma on split lists (new style), as demonstrated in feature3 and feature4 test cases.

Feature 7 (selection_tracking): when `selection_start` and `selection_length` are in the JSON input, the adapter emits the `text<<END ... END selection_start=N selection_length=N` block format to stdout.

context_pointers.vague_reference: a utility for splitting source into before/selected/after segments
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 8 (source_segments): `operation=source_segments` with optional `selection_start`/`selection_length`, outputting `before=`, `selected=`, `after=` lines or an `error=invalid_selection` block.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect. Please check the existing codebase structure and the test case JSON files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` for authoritative examples of expected input/output behavior, and refer to the dart_style package documentation for underlying formatter capabilities.

feature1_file_unit_formatting.json.description: Formats whole source files by normalizing whitespace, preserving comments, and ensuring file output ends with exactly one newline.

feature2_statement_formatting.json.description: Formats a single statement or expression fragment without adding a file-level trailing newline, while supporting caller-provided leading indentation.

feature3_width_and_style_splitting.json.description: Splits or keeps argument and collection layouts according to the selected formatting style and configured page width.

input.source:
method(first, second, third, fourth, fifth, sixth, seventh, eighth, ninth,
    tenth, eleventh, twelfth);

cases.expected_output:
method(
    first,
    second,
    third,
    fourth,
    fifth,
    sixth,
    seventh,
    eighth,
    ninth,
    tenth,
    eleventh,
    twelfth);

feature4_language_version_behavior.json.description: Uses the configured language version and any leading source version comment to select syntax support and versioned formatting behavior.

feature5_line_endings.json.description: Chooses output line separators from an explicit option or from the first line ending found in the input, including multiline string handling.

feature6_trailing_comma_policy.json.description: Applies the requested trailing-comma policy so existing commas can force multiline layouts when preservation is requested, while comma-free inputs may remain compact.

feature7_selection_tracking.json.description: Returns formatted text together with the adjusted selected range when a caller supplies a source selection.

cases.expected_output:
text<<END
a + first + second;
END
selection_start=5
selection_length=10

feature8_source_range_segments.json.description: Exposes the text before, inside, and after an optional selected range, and rejects ranges whose bounds are incomplete or outside the source text.

feature9_error_reporting.json.description: Reports parse failures and formatter safety-check failures using normalized, language-neutral stdout categories.

input.source:
var a = some error;

      var b = another one;
